title: Support Vector Machines
overview: Covers the core SVM idea from IB031: why maximum margin is a good goal, how the margin is defined geometrically, and what support vectors are. Quadratic programming and kernel methods are not included.
creator: ib031-study

cards.question: Among all linear classifiers that correctly separate the training data, SVM selects the one that:
options: Maximises the margin between the two classes
Maximises the dot product of the weight vector with each support vector
cards.explanation: The SVM idea is to prefer the maximum-margin classifier: out of all weight vectors consistent with the training set, choose the one whose separating hyperplane is as far as possible from the nearest training points of both classes.

cards.question: What are 'support vectors' in the context of SVM?
options: All training examples, weighted by their distance to the hyperplane
The training examples that lie on the margin boundary
The weight vectors that define the hyperplane
The misclassified examples that are closest to the decision boundary
cards.explanation: Support vectors are the training points that sit on the margin (i.e., are closest to the separating hyperplane). They are the only examples that influence the position of the hyperplane — all others could be removed without changing the solution.

cards.question: In SVM, the distance of a feature vector x from the separating hyperplane defined by weight vector w is:
cards.explanation: The signed distance from x to the hyperplane is w·x̃ / ||w||, and the unsigned distance is |w·x̃| / ||w||. Here x̃ = (1, x1, ..., xn) is the augmented feature vector (with the bias input x0 = 1 prepended).

cards.prompt: The margin of a weight vector w with respect to a training set D is defined as ___ times the minimum distance between any training point and the separating hyperplane.
cards.explanation: The margin is 2 * min_k d[w](x_k). The factor 2 comes from counting the distance on both sides of the hyperplane (one for each class). SVM maximises this quantity.

cards.question: In the standard SVM formulation, class labels are encoded as:
cards.explanation: SVM uses y_k ∈ {-1, 1}. The constraints for a consistent classifier are then: w·x̃_k > 0 whenever y_k = 1, and w·x̃_k < 0 whenever y_k = -1. This is different from the {0, 1} labelling used in logistic regression.

cards.question: Which of the following correctly states the SVM optimisation goal?
options: Find w that minimises ||w|| subject to w being consistent with D
Find w consistent with D that maximises min_k |w · x̃_k| / ||w||
Find w that maximises the sum of distances of all training points to the hyperplane
Find w that minimises the squared error on the training set
cards.explanation: We want the weight vector w, consistent with the training set D, that maximises the minimum distance of any training point to the separating hyperplane — that is, maximises min_k |w·x̃_k| / ||w||. This is equivalent to maximising the margin.

cards.question: Explain in your own words why choosing the maximum-margin classifier is intuitively a good idea for generalisation.
cards.explanation: A larger margin means the hyperplane is far from all training points on both sides. This gives more 'room' for new data: a test point can deviate from the training distribution somewhat without being misclassified. Intuitively, the maximum-margin classifier is the most robust linear separator — it is least likely to be fooled by small perturbations in the data. Only the support vectors (the points on the margin) matter; moving any non-support-vector training example would not change the solution.
